planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; intermediate fothergilla is a hybrid between F. gardenii and F. major and is best propagated by softwood cuttings. The cultivar 'Blue Shadow' is most widely available and prized for its intense blue-green summer foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; intermediate fothergilla is a naturally occurring and cultivated hybrid between small fothergilla (F. gardenii) and large fothergilla (F. major), combining the best traits of both parents — the compact habit of the dwarf parent with the larger flower heads and superior fall color of the tall parent. The fragrant, bottlebrush-like white flowers emerge before the leaves in early spring, offering a spectacular display of creamy puffball blooms on bare stems. The summer foliage of the cultivar 'Blue Shadow' is an unusually intense blue-green — among the most striking blue tones of any deciduous shrub — before turning a fiery combination of red, orange, and yellow in fall. A superb specimen shrub for woodland gardens, foundation plantings, and native shrub borders in zones 4–8, combining especially well with azaleas, mountain laurels, and other acid-loving shrubs.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; winter hazels are best in sheltered positions where late frosts will not damage the early blooms. Propagate by softwood cuttings in early summer.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade with shelter from late-season frosts; buttercup winter hazel is the smallest and most refined of the Corylopsis species, producing delicate, drooping racemes of pale yellow, primrose-scented flowers in late winter or very early spring — among the earliest ornamental shrubs to bloom, often appearing in late February or March alongside snowdrops and before forsythia. The small flowers are each shaped like a tiny cowslip, nodding in clusters on bare winter stems. The heart-shaped, finely toothed leaves emerge bronze-green after flowering and mature to fresh green with fine veining. More compact than other winter hazels, it is well suited to smaller gardens and shaded woodland edges where its subtle, early charm can be appreciated up close. Site in a sheltered spot to protect blooms from late freezes.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Chinese winter hazel benefits from a sheltered position to protect early blooms from late frosts. Grows to a larger multi-stemmed shrub than dwarf winter hazel.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Chinese winter hazel is a large, multi-stemmed shrub native to mountain forests of central China, producing long, pendulous racemes of soft yellow flowers with a light, sweet fragrance in late winter or very early spring before the leaves emerge. Each drooping chain of 15–20 small flowers resembles a string of yellow bells, and a well-established shrub in full bloom creates a spectacular curtain of pale gold in the February garden. The large, boldly veined, heart-shaped leaves that follow are attractive through summer, turning yellow in fall. One of the more cold-tolerant Corylopsis species, surviving reliably to zone 6 and worth trying in sheltered zone 5 microclimates. The variety var. calvescens 'Spring Purple' has purple-flushed new growth; 'Wisley Red' has red-tinged new leaves and calyces.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; spike winter hazel is among the most widely available Corylopsis species in US nurseries and is known for unusually long, many-flowered racemes. Site in a sheltered location to protect from late winter frosts.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade, sheltered from late winter frosts; spike winter hazel is a Japanese species distinguished from other Corylopsis by the unusually long, cylindrical racemes containing up to 20 lemon-yellow flowers each — the longest pendulous flower clusters of commonly grown winter hazels. The flowers are slightly larger than those of C. pauciflora and carry a soft, sweet, cowslip-like fragrance that is particularly pleasant in the mild days of late winter and early spring. The purple-tipped stamens create a colorful contrast with the pale yellow petals. Leaves emerge with an attractive reddish-purple flush that matures to blue-green, turning golden yellow in fall. Hardy to zone 5, this is one of the more reliable winter hazels for cold-climate gardens. Combines beautifully with early-blooming witch hazels (Hamamelis) for an extended winter flowering display.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Walter's dogwood is an underused Chinese species valued for its strong trunk character, reliable flowering, and excellent drought tolerance once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Walter's dogwood is a large shrub or small tree from central and northern China distinguished among dogwoods by its striking deeply furrowed, interlacing bark — among the most dramatically textured trunks of any dogwood — and by notable drought tolerance once established. The flat-topped clusters of small, creamy-white flowers in early summer are followed by blue-black berries consumed by birds. Like other Cornelian-cherry relatives, the fall color is an attractive red-purple. Significantly more drought and heat tolerant than flowering dogwood (C. florida) and less susceptible to dogwood anthracnose, making it a more durable choice for Southern gardens in zones 7–9 where C. florida often struggles. An underused specimen tree that deserves wider planting for its ornamental bark and adaptability.
